serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Lamb Vindaloo is approximately 250 g, comprising lamb pieces and potatoes in vindaloo sauce, usually accompanied by rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb Vindaloo — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Lamb Vindaloo (cr_id G00010474) with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values are sourced from this canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat and Vegetable Curry Dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for curry-based dishes with potato and legume components
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Lamb Vindaloo in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated by analogy to mixed meat-and-potato curry dishes (GI range 40–50) documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. The vinegar-based marinade characteristic of vindaloo is known to further reduce postprandial glucose response (Liljeberg & Bjorck, 1998, AJCN). Glycemic load calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g per serving / 100 = 45 × 30.8 / 100 ≈ 13.8 (based on 250 g serving providing 30.8 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 383 mg/100 g (borderline range), partially offset by potassium 292 mg/100 g and dietary fibre 3.4 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation to bring sodium below 300 mg/100 g; the high potassium-to-sodium ratio and dietary fibre content provide partial cardiovascular benefit, but portion control to 150–200 g is advisable to limit total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~45), high dietary fibre 3.4 g/100 g moderating glucose absorption, vinegar marinade further attenuating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb Vindaloo has a low estimated glycemic index and a favourable fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should pair it with a low-GI grain such as brown rice or whole-grain bread and confirm no added sugar in the sauce to maintain glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lamb is a red meat with saturated fat content (exact value not reported in HPB source); sodium at 383 mg/100 g is borderline; anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, cumin) and vinegar provide partial cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 150 g, request lean lamb cuts and reduced oil in preparation, and note that the anti-inflammatory spice blend (turmeric, cumin, ginger) and vinegar marinade offer partial cardioprotective benefit, but the red-meat base and borderline sodium warrant moderation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6); nightshade ingredients (tomato, chili) present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Lamb Vindaloo due to the arachidonic acid burden of red meat and the presence of nightshade vegetables; substituting lamb with chicken or chickpeas retains the anti-inflammatory spice benefits without the primary RA disqualifier.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb Vindaloo is a fiery, tangy Indian curry originating from Goa, India, where Portuguese culinary influence introduced vinegar-based meat marinades that became a hallmark of the dish. It is prepared by marinating lamb pieces in a paste of vinegar, chili, garlic, ginger, turmeric, cumin, mustard seeds, cinnamon, and cloves, then slow-cooking the marinated meat with onions, tomatoes, and potatoes until the lamb is tender and the sauce is deeply reduced and intensely spiced. The dish has a bold, hot, and sour flavour profile with a rich, thick sauce and tender lamb pieces. In Singapore, Lamb Vindaloo is served in Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as a lunch or dinner dish, typically accompanied by white rice, roti prata, or naan. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 111 kcal, 3.68 g protein, 5.65 g fat, 12.33 g carbohydrate, 3.4 g dietary fibre, and 383 mg sodium. The high dietary fibre content relative to carbohydrate, combined with the vinegar marinade, results in a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45, making it a moderate glycaemic choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when paired with a low-GI grain. The borderline sodium level warrants caution for individuals with hypertension, though the potassium content of 292 mg/100 g provides partial offsetting benefit. The red-meat base contributes arachidonic acid, making the dish unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. The anti-inflammatory spice blend — particularly turmeric (curcumin) and ginger — provides partial cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory benefit.

How many calories in lamb vindaloo?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database canonical entry for Lamb Vindaloo (cr_id G00010474) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The HPB is a primary approved source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented as such.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this Lamb Vindaloo preparation are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry (G00010474) as of 2026-04-16. No supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN) provides an exact match for this specific Singapore-style preparation. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is accepted as confirmed zero per the HPB canonical record, though it is nutritionally atypical for a lamb dish and may reflect the specific preparation method or a reporting convention in the HPB database. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed meat-and-potato curry dishes; no direct GI measurement for Lamb Vindaloo was found. 4. The description in the input JSON contains a typographical error ('pitatoes' should be 'potatoes'); corrected in this record. 5. Halal status is marked true as lamb is a halal-permissible meat; however, halal certification of the specific preparation cannot be confirmed from the HPB record alone.
